speaker: patient
text: Hi, Doctor. I've been feeling a bit lightheaded when I stand up quickly lately.

speaker: doctor
text: Thanks for coming in. Based on your recent evaluation, your primary diagnosis is Essential Hypertension, confirmed on February 27th.

speaker: doctor
text: Your hemoglobin level was 175 g/L on that same date — well within the normal range for adult males.

speaker: doctor
text: Your diastolic blood pressure reading that day was 60 mmHg, which is on the lower end but not alarming yet.

speaker: patient
text: Oh, okay. I’ve been checking my BP at home — sometimes it feels low, especially in the mornings.

speaker: doctor
text: That’s helpful to know. Let’s review your full set of vitals from the visit. Your systolic pressure was recorded at 132 mmHg on February 27th.

speaker: patient
text: Wait — I think I saw a higher number on my chart last time. Wasn’t it 143?

speaker: doctor
text: You're right — we did record a systolic of 143 mmHg earlier that same day during triage. That variation can happen due to stress or activity before measurement.

speaker: doctor
text: You’re currently on Metformin 500mg twice daily, started on February 27th, primarily for prediabetes management alongside your hypertension.

speaker: patient
text: Yes, I’ve been taking it consistently. My fasting sugars have improved, but I’m still noticing dizziness.

speaker: doctor
text: Given the fluctuating readings and your symptoms, let’s double-check your diastolic pressure — it was 60 mmHg again during our repeat measurement later that day.

speaker: patient
text: That matches what I’ve been seeing at home — often around 60.

speaker: doctor
text: Also, your hemoglobin came back at 180 g/L on the same lab draw — slightly elevated from the earlier 175 g/L, possibly reflecting mild hemoconcentration or assay variation.

speaker: patient
text: Hmm, interesting. I didn’t realize it had gone up — is that something to worry about?

speaker: doctor
text: Not acutely — but given your persistent low diastolic pressures and symptom burden, I’d like to adjust your Metformin dose to improve metabolic control more robustly.

speaker: doctor
text: I am going to switch you to Metformin 1000mg twice daily, starting tomorrow. We’ll monitor for GI tolerance and reassess your BP trends next week.

speaker: patient
text: Okay, I’ll start that. Should I keep tracking my BP at home?

speaker: doctor
text: Yes — especially the diastolic value. Please note the time and position — seated, rested for five minutes.

speaker: patient
text: Will do. Actually, just now — right before coming in — I checked and got a diastolic of 60 mmHg again, same as that reading from the visit.

speaker: doctor
text: Thanks for confirming that — it reinforces the pattern we saw. Let’s schedule a follow-up in seven days to assess response and review any side effects from the new dose.
